headlines.title: At least 9 arrested, 77 vehicles impounded following Bay Bridge takeover
headlines.description: Authorities have arrested at least nine people and impounded 77 vehicles in connection with Sunday’s takeover on the Bay Bridge, officials announced Monday. In videos shared across multiple social media platforms, dozens of dirt bike and ATV riders could be seen doing stunts on the upper deck of the bridge during the illegal stunt show. On Monday, Oakland police Chief James Beere said his officers had been investigating the group of riders for the last couple months, and they were ready to take action when the group showed up over the weekend. “The planned operation involved more than 100 additional officers from multiple Oakland departments and units,” Beere said. Beere said his officers tracked the group’s movements, beginning with a stop in San Leandro and followed by a stop in East Oakland to unload motorbikes and ATVs. The group then rode to Lake Merritt, Berkeley, over the bridge to San Francisco and back, creating chaos along the way. According to San Francisco police, the group arrived in the city at about 4:45 p.m. “This lasted over 45 minutes, and they were in our downtown area and Ingleside districts,” San Francisco police Chief Derrick Lew said. “They quickly overwhelmed intersections and caused great risk to public safety.” As the group headed back to Oakland and approached the Bay Bridge toll plaza, Oakland police, California Highway Patrol and San Francisco police jumped into action and started to make arrests. Videos posted to multiple social media platforms showed some riders abandoning their vehicles and running. Nine people in total were arrested. Five can expect misdemeanor charges, while four more face felonies. “During this operation, one individual attempted to flee on foot, jumped a fence and decided to dive into the water,” Beere said. According to the Oakland Fire Department, that person was rescued and then arrested. In addition to the arrests on the bridge, Beere said officers also arrested two promoters before the ride and they impounded the vehicles used to haul all of the motorbikes and ATVs to Oakland. For those who managed to get away, Beere said authorities are working to track them down and take them into custody.

headlines.title: Illegal gambling operation at San Jose home busted again
headlines.description: Authorities arrested a suspect who allegedly kept an illegal gambling den running out of a San Jose home after it was busted by police four months ago. The first arrests came on Dec. 30, when police took four suspects into custody at the home in the 2700 block of Cramer Circle. Several gambling machines and narcotics were seized as well, San Jose police said Monday. Authorities discovered the operation had continued, and on April 7 another search warrant was served at the home and more gambling machines were seized. Officers identified the primary suspect as 26-year-old Deon Nguyen, obtaining a warrant for his arrest and a search warrant for his home. He was arrested Thursday in San Jose and during a search, police allegedly found seven rifles with various illegal modifications including a short-barreled fully automatic rifle, a shotgun, and 10 handguns, police said in a news release. In addition, police allegedly seized several extended magazines, thousands of rounds of ammunition, ghost gun manufacturing parts, butterfly knives, brass knuckles, about $51,000 in cash and gold bullion. San Jose 9 hours ago San Jose 11 hours ago 1 injured in house fire in South San Jose

headlines.title: Doris Fisher, Gap co-founder who helped reshape US casual style, dies at 94
headlines.description: Doris Fisher, who co-founded the iconic clothing chain The Gap Inc. in 1969 with her late husband Don, has died. She was 94. Fisher died on Saturday surrounded by her family, a Gap spokesperson confirmed on Monday. The San Francisco-based company did not specify a cause of death. The couple co-founded The Gap after a frustrating shopping experience when Don Fisher couldn’t find a pair of jeans that fit, according to the retailer. The Fishers opened one small store on Ocean Avenue in San Francisco. Initially men’s Levi’s jeans and record tapes were the only items for sale. But the brand became the foundation for a global retail empire and reshaped American fashion with a focus on simple casual looks from khakis and jeans to T-shirts and sweater sets. The chain eventually expanded to other nameplates — Banana Republic and Old Navy — and now generates more than $15 billion in sales globally. Fisher was the company’s fashion merchandiser for nearly four decades, while her husband focused on the business side. The company said that she came up with the company’s name, specifically to bridge the “generation gap” between parents and children. Don Fisher died in 2009. “There is simply no equal to Doris Fisher,” Gap’s CEO and President Richard Dickson said in a statement issued Monday. “In Gap-speak, she was a true original. Doris was a full partner in Gap Inc.’s founding and a path-breaking entrepreneur at a time when that was highly unusual for women. She understood firsthand the value of self-expression, diversity, and inclusion.” Dickson, who has been spearheading a turnaround after several years of a sales malaise, also said that Doris Fisher “worked tirelessly to ensure that Gap Inc. always did more than sell clothes.” Gap hired Dickson in July 2023. Gap noted that Doris Fisher’s influence extended beyond merchandising and store design. She helped shape the cultural tone of Gap advertising and product development, while maintaining a “steadfast” presence in the company’s expansion and pushing the company to focus on the customer’s needs. Gap went public in 1976. The Fishers also were involved in philanthropic endeavors. The couple amassed one of the largest private collections of modern and contemporary art in the country, Gap said. In 2009, the family pledged more than 1,100 works to the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, one of the largest gifts of its kind. Doris Fisher was also an advocate of educational opportunities for disadvantaged students. She served on the board of Knowledge Is Power Program, known as KIPP, a network of high-performing charter schools aimed at creating opportunity for underserved students. Born in San Francisco in 1931, Doris Feigenbaum grew up in a family “steeped in values of enterprise, culture, and community service,” according to Gap. She graduated from Stanford University in 1953, where she studied economics. She is survived by her three sons — Robert, William, and John — all of whom have carried forward the family’s business and philanthropic commitments with San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, KIPP, Stanford University, The Boys & Girls Club of San Francisco, the San Francisco Symphony, and The Gap Foundation. She is also survived by 10 grandchildren and 13 great grandchildren, the company said.

headlines.title: OpenAI president discloses his stake in the company is worth $30B
headlines.description: Greg Brockman, OpenAI’s president and CEO Sam Altman’s top lieutenant, disclosed in court Monday that his stake in the artificial intelligence company is worth nearly $30 billion. Brockman, who also said he did not personally invest any money in OpenAI, was testifying Monday in the trial that centers on the company’s 2015 founding as a nonprofit startup primarily funded by Elon Musk before evolving into a capitalistic venture now valued at $852 billion. Brockman’s disclosure would put him on the Forbes list of the world’s richest people, with wealth comparable to Melinda French Gates. The civil lawsuit accuses Altman and Brockman of double-crossing Musk by straying from the San Francisco company’s founding mission to be an altruistic steward of a revolutionary technology. The lawsuit alleges they shifted into a moneymaking mode behind Musk’s back. Late Sunday, OpenAI lawyers tried to admit as evidence a text message Musk sent to Brockman two days before the trial began. According to a court filing — which did not include the actual text exchange — Musk sent a message to Brockman to gauge interest in settlement. When Brockman replied that both sides should drop their respective claims, Musk shot back, according to the filing, “By the end of this week, you and Sam will be the most hated men in America. If you insist, so it will be.” Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers, who is overseeing the trial, did not admit the text exchange as evidence. Artificial intelligence 11 hours ago Elon Musk Apr 30 Elon Musk spars with OpenAI attorney in trial over company's evolution from a nonprofit

headlines.title: 2026 WNBA franchise valuations: Golden State Valkyries are first $1 billion team
headlines.description: The WNBA has its first $1 billion franchise. And that franchise just started play one season ago. CNBC released its ranking of the most valuable WNBA franchises in 2026, and it’s the Golden State Valkyries that top the list with a $1 billion valuation. After Golden State, the top five are the New York Liberty ($600 million), Indiana Fever ($560 million), Las Vegas Aces ($500 million) and Seattle Storm ($450 million). So, how does a new team like the Valkyries skyrocket to the top of the ranking in just one year? CNBC senior sports reporter Michael Ozanian broke it down. “Their revenue for the 2025 season was $73 million. In 2026, their revenue could exceed $90 million,” Ozanian said. “The owners of the Valkyrie, Peter Goober and Joel LeCoe, they also own the Golden State Warriors of the NBA and they own the Chase Center where both the Valkyries and the Warriors play. This has given them a tremendous opportunity to cross sell sponsorships, to cross sell hospitality at the arena. MORE WNBA COVERAGE WNBA Apr 22 WNBA Apr 13 WNBA Apr 9 “They have tremendous scale at the building between the two teams and they’ve done a great job marketing the team. They really are a prime example of the fact that in the WNBA, the teams that tend to be the most valuable are those that play in NBA sized arenas and are also owned by people who own NBA teams.” Ozanian added that Golden State is set to have 12,000 season tickets sold this season, which would be a league record. Ozanian also predicts that within the next five years, all well-run WNBA teams will reach the billion-dollar valuation. New owners coming in have invested significant money into their teams and are finding unique ways to boost profit. Star youngsters like Caitlin Clark, Angel Reese and Paige Bueckers, among others, play a crucial role in growing interest, too. Here’s the full 15-team list… This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: California counties send out ballots for June 2 primary election
headlines.description: Registered California voters should soon see their ballots for the June 2 primary election arriving in the mailbox. Monday was the last day for counties across California to start sending out the ballots to all active, registered voters, according to California Secretary of State Shirley N. Weber. Secure ballot drop-off locations will open on Tuesday, and ballots can also be taken to a county elections office or returned by mail using the ballot’s postage-paid return envelope. Vote-by-mail ballots must be postmarked no later than June 2, and anyone mailing a ballot on Election Day should get a hand-stamped postmark from a postal employee inside a U.S. Post Office. The last day to register to vote for the June 2 primary election is May 18. On June 2, polls will be open from 7 a.m. to 8 p.m. Election guides were mailed last week and should have already arrived in voters’ mailboxes. More information on the upcoming election can be found at sos.ca.gov. California 11 hours ago Decision 2026 Apr 16

headlines.title: Cause of deadly chain-reaction crash in San Jose remains under investigation
headlines.description: San Jose police on Monday said they were still trying to determine the cause of Saturday’s deadly chain-reaction crash near Oakridge mall. The multi-vehicle collision happened just before 4 p.m. in the area of Santa Teresa Boulevard and Blossom Hill Road, police said. Citing a preliminary investigation, police said a man behind the wheel of a Mercedes sedan was heading southbound on Santa Teresa Boulevard when he hit a Kia sedan. The Kia, driven by a woman, was also heading southbound and in the process of moving into the dedicated left turn lane for eastbound Blossom Hill Road. The Mercedes and Kia both slammed into a Tesla sedan carrying three people – adult male driver, adult female passenger and juvenile passenger – that was stopped in the left turn lane, according to police. The impact caused the Tesla to hit a BMW sedan, driven by a man, directly in front of it. The Kia also hit a Toyota sedan, driven by a woman, that was stopped in a second left turn lane. San Jose May 2 San Jose 11 hours ago Following the chain-reaction wreck, the Mercedes caught fire with the driver and his juvenile passenger still inside, police said. Responding officers quickly pulled both of them out of the car. Everyone involved in the collision, except for the man driving the BMW, was transported to local hospitals with varying injuries, police said. The woman driving the Kia died at the hospital, according to police. Her identity will be released by the county’s medical examiner office. The driver of the Mercedes has been cooperative with investigators, police said. While the cause of the collision remains under investigation, police said alcohol and drugs do not appear to have played a roll. “We do have some indication that the initial driver may have been experiencing some sort of medical emergency,” San Jose police spokesperson Stacie Shih said. “We’re not sure yet. We’re trying to figure that out.” This marked San Jose’s 18th deadly collision of 2026, according to police. Anyone with information regarding the crash is encouraged to contact San Jose police Detective DelliCarpini by email at 4103@sanjoseca.gov or by phone at 408-277-4654.

headlines.title: Oakland's Alysa Liu named Louis Vuitton ambassador
headlines.description: Two-time Olympic gold medal figure skater Alysa Liu is stepping into the world of high fashion. The Oakland native has been named a Louis Vuitton ambassador, sharing a video and images from a recent photoshoot with the luxury brand. The collaboration comes as little surprise. Liu was seen wearing Louis Vuitton during Paris Fashion Week just days after winning gold at the Milan Cortina Games in February. Liu is one of the most prominent athletes from the Bay Area and has gained international attention both on and off the ice. Olympics Mar 24 Oakland Mar 17 Oakland Apr 17 Artist unveils mural honoring Alysa Liu, Kristi Yamaguchi in Oakland

headlines.title: San Francisco woman gets photographer's old number. It changes both their lives
headlines.description: Sometimes, even a wrong number can make the right connection. That was the case for Lauren Stevens, whose newly assigned work phone came with an unexpected problem: it kept receiving calls and texts for someone named “Verndawg.” At first, the messages were confusing. Over time, they became life-changing, for both Stevens and the man behind the nickname, renowned San Francisco photographer Wernher Krutein. Shortly after getting the phone, Stevens began receiving repeated messages clearly meant for someone else. Curious, she and a group of friends did some online sleuthing to track down the intended recipient. It didn’t take long. “One of my friends texted me and said, ‘This guy, Wernher Krutein, he’s iconic,’” Stevens said. Krutein, 72, has spent more than six decades traveling the world with a camera, documenting everything from people and architecture to insects and everyday objects. While his work spans continents, some of his most famous photographs were taken closer to home in San Francisco. One image immediately stood out to Stevens: Krutein’s striking photograph from the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake, showing a car dangling from the collapsed Bay Bridge. “I grew up seeing that photo in textbooks and documentaries,” she said. “And this was the photo.” A longtime film photography enthusiast herself, Stevens found Krutein’s old website, Photovault.com, an archive of nearly half a million images, and decided to email him. What began as a practical exchange about forwarding messages soon became something deeper. “You could tell he didn’t really care about the messages,” Stevens said. “What mattered to him was that someone cared about his work.” Emails turned into phone calls, then in-person visits to Krutein’s home in Sonoma County. As their friendship grew, Stevens began to understand the challenges the celebrated photographer was quietly facing. Starting his website in the late 1990s, Krutein was once ahead of the technological curve, but in an increasingly digital world, he found himself struggling financially. “I’ve been barely making a living for years,” Krutein said. “I’ve pared down everything. I don’t even have heat in the house.” He told Stevens his savings could last anywhere from six months to two years. After that, he wasn’t sure what would happen. Wanting to help, Stevens launched a GoFundMe campaign to assist with Krutein’s living expenses. It has since raised more than $15,000. But financial relief was only part of her goal. Krutein was deeply worried about the future of his archive, his life’s work, and, as he describes it, his purpose. “I love connecting with everything I photograph,” he said. “Bugs, cars, people, furniture, all of it fascinates me.” Using her understanding of social media, Stevens created TikTok and Instagram accounts called @Verndawgtales, documenting their friendship and sharing Krutein’s photographs and stories. Thousands of followers now track their journey as Stevens works to preserve Krutein’s legacy and bring renewed attention to his work. “The world needs to see his work,” Stevens said. “It is crazy, diabolically amazing, and I feel so lucky to be the vessel to share his story.” For Krutein, the impact has gone beyond recognition or financial support. “She’s brought me out of the darkness,” he said. “That’s a gift beyond words.”

headlines.title: 1 injured in house fire in South San Jose
headlines.description: Firefighters knocked down a house fire that injured one resident on Monday morning in South San Jose. The blaze was reported about 8:30 a.m. at a one-story home in the 4300 block of Indigo Drive, according to the San Jose Fire Department. Fire crews had the fire knocked down by 8:42 a.m. and officials said one person was transported to a hospital with non-life-threatening injuries.

headlines.title: SF leaders back bill to expand sober housing funding
headlines.description: San Francisco leaders are backing state legislation they said could help address a major gap in the city’s response to the drug crisis. At an event Monday morning, Mayor Daniel Lurie joined state Assemblymember Matt Haney and Bay Area Council CEO Libby Schaaf in support of Assembly Bill 1556, which is currently under consideration in Sacramento. The bill would allow state funding to be used for sober living housing, something city leaders said currently is restricted. “With all of the work we are doing, there is still a major gap [in] sober housing,” Lurie said. “Right now, state funding cannot be used. This bill will fix that.” Supporters claim expanding funding could increase access to drug-free housing for people working toward recovery. Schaaf said the lack of those options has impacted both communities and people struggling with addiction. Leaders pointed to programs like Wells Place in San Francisco as an example of temporary sober supportive housing already in use. The bill remains at the committee level. San Francisco 13 hours ago San Francisco Feb 17 San Francisco Jan 7 San Francisco to address drug crisis with RESET Center

headlines.title: OpenAI President Greg Brockman takes stand in Elon Musk vs. Sam Altman trial
headlines.description: What to Know Elon Musk has filed a $134 billion lawsuit against Sam Altman and his top lieutenant, Greg Brockman, along with Microsoft over its investments in OpenAI, in 2024. Musk claims OpenAI violated its original mission to operate as a nonprofit focused on developing artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity. OpenAI has brushed off Musk’s allegations as a case of sour grapes aimed at undercutting its rapid growth and bolstering Musk’s own xAI, which he launched in 2023 as a competitor. The nine-person jury was selected on April 27. The trial is being held in Oakland federal court and scheduled to continue through late May. Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers excused Musk from the witness stand Thursday, but he may be called back later. Editor’s note: This live blog has ended and is no longer being updated. The landmark OpenAI trial resumes Monday after Elon Musk on Thursday sparred with an attorney for the company during his third day of testimony. Court was not in session Friday. The trial centers on the 2015 birth of the ChatGPT maker as a nonprofit startup primarily funded by Musk before evolving into a capitalistic venture now valued at $852 billion. It pits the world’s richest person against Sam Altman, a fellow OpenAI co-founder he accuses of betraying promises to keep the company as a nonprofit dedicated to humanity’s benefit. NBC Bay Area business and tech reporter Scott Budman is at the federal courthouse in Oakland and providing live updates below.
headlines.source: Scott Budman, Kristofer Noceda, Joel Soria and Brendan Weber
